item.warfare_wings.b17.description: A four-engine heavy bomber developed by the American Boeing Company, it first flew in 1935. It had a maximum speed of about 462 km/h and a range of about 3,200 km. After the war, some were still used as transport and reconnaissance aircraft.

item.warfare_wings.b29.description: A high-performance four-engine heavy bomber developed by Boeing and fielded in 1944. Equipped with advanced technologies such as a pressurized cabin, remote-controlled gun turrets, and radar bombing sights, it was also converted to an aerial refueling aircraft after the war.

item.warfare_wings.f4u.description: A shipboard fighter developed by the American company Chance Vought, it was fielded in 1942. With its powerful firepower and high acceleration performance, it is considered one of the most successful ship-based fighters of the Allied Forces.

item.warfare_wings.sbd.description: A dive bomber developed by the American company Douglas, it began operation in 1940. With its sturdy structure and precision bombing capability, it was instrumental in the Battle of Midway, sinking four Japanese aircraft carriers and turning the tide of the war.

item.warfare_wings.d4y.description: The D4Y was a naval bomber operated by the Imperial Japanese Navy. The early models were powered by German-derived liquid-cooled engines and had excellent high-speed and aerodynamic performance, but they had maintenance and reliability issues, and the later models were switched to air-cooled engines.

item.warfare_wings.g4m.description: Developed by Mitsubishi Heavy Industries, this twin-engine land attack aircraft of the Imperial Japanese Navy was used for lightning, bombing, and reconnaissance missions. It is also known as the aircraft in which Admiral Isoroku Yamamoto was shot down while on board.

item.warfare_wings.g10n1.description: The G10N Fugaku was a super-large strategic bomber conceived by Nakajima Aircraft Company, and was the core aircraft of the “Z Aircraft Concept,” which aimed at bombing the U.S. mainland, but the project was cancelled due to the deteriorating war situation and lack of materials. Fugaku TypeI is a fictitious model named by our contents.

item.warfare_wings.il2.description: Developed by the Soviet Union's Ilyushin Design Bureau, this ground attack aircraft was called a “flying tank” because of its heavy armor and powerful firepower. It achieved great success in the German-Soviet War, and with a total production of 36,183 aircraft, it is the most produced military aircraft in history.

item.warfare_wings.he111.description: A twin-engine bomber developed by the German company Heinkel. With its elliptical wings and unique bomb bay structure, it was widely operated as a medium-range bomber in World War II, but its defensive capabilities and speed were noticeably lacking in the latter stages of the war.
